Motion To Reclassify Action From Unlimited To Limited
Off Calendar Jun-25-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant's motion to reclassify is denied with prejudice.

"In ruling upon a request to transfer a civil action to the municipal court, the superior court is not permitted to try the merits of the action. Instead, the superior court is limited to determining whether the action will necessarily result in a verdict below its jurisdictional amount." (Singer v. Superior Court (1999) 70 Cal.App.4th 1315, 1317 (emphasis in original); see also Ytuarte v. Superior Court (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 266, 277 ["Accordingly under Walker the superior court must deny the motion to reclassify the case as limited (and thus keep the matter in the unlimited civil court) unless it appears to a legal certainty that the plaintiff's damages will necessarily be less than $25,000. This standard of 'legal certainty' is not met when it appears a verdict within the unlimited court's jurisdiction is 'possible.'"].)

In this case, defendant fails to meet this heavy burden. Plaintiff alleges a claim for intentional misrepresentation and the court overruled defendant's demurrer to this claim. Nothing in the record indicates that the claim will necessarily fail and the limitation of liability clause in the parties' contract does not bar tort damages. (Food Safety Net Services v. Eco Safe Systems USA, Inc. (2012) 209 Cal.App.4th 1118, 1126 ["[L]imitation of liability clauses are ineffective with respect to claims for fraud and misrepresentation."].) Based on the sparse record before the court, it would be an abuse of discretion to grant this motion.

Plaintiff's request for sanctions is denied.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Reclassify Action From Unlimited To Limited Jurisdiction
Off Calendar Feb-20-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. SUSANNA CHENETTE, Esq ( 415-261-8527) for Plaintiffs.
Atty. VICTORIA ODELL, Esq. of Capobianco Law Offices, P.C. (Tel: 760-568-6500) for Defendant.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT HERITAGE GLOBAL PARTNERS, INC.'s Motion To Reclassify Action From Unlimited To Limited Jurisdiction.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with the modification to deny the motion without prejudice.

Defendant Heritage Global Partners, Inc.'s ("HGP") motion to reclassify is denied.

Plaintiffs Stacy Young and Yassin Olabi purchased $7,627.90 worth of Twitter-related items at an auction run by HGP. HGP allegedly prevented them from obtaining some of their purchased goods. (Compl. para. 38, 48.) Plaintiffs sued HGP for contract and tort claims, among other causes of action, designating the case as an unlimited civil case. HGP now moves to reclassify the action as limited under CCP 403.040(a).

CCP 403.040(a) enables a party to move to reclassify an unlimited action as limited if the amount in controversy does not exceed $25,000. (See CCP 85 [Here, the parties fail to address potential application of an amendment to the amount in controversy requirement, effective January 1, 2024].) In other words, the court must deny a motion to reclassify a case as limited unless it appears to a legal certainty plaintiffs' damages will necessarily be less than $25,000. (Ytuarte v. Superior Court (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 266, 277, citing Walker v. Superior Court (1991) 53 Cal.3d 257.) The court looks to the record to determine whether the result is obtainable. (Id. at 279.) The prayer for relief is relevant to valuation of the amount in controversy. (Stern v. Superior Court (2003) 105 Cal.App.4th 223, 233.)

Here, HGP argues that most of plaintiffs' prayers for relief should be disregarded because the non-contractual claims "are improper for the reasons argued in Defendant's concurrently filed demurrer and motion to strike." (Mot. 5:11-13.) This is not the proper vehicle to consider the merits of plaintiffs' non-contractual claims. (See Singer v. Superior Court (1999) 70 Cal.App.4th 1315, 1317 [In determining whether an action is unlimited or limited, "the superior court is not permitted to try the merits of the action"].) The court has not yet ruled on any motions attacking the pleadings, and it cannot be said these claims will fail as a matter of law. Plaintiffs in their complaint seek over $225,000 in damages based on the estimated resale value of their goods, and civil penalties for violations of Bus. & Prof. Code section 17206. Their prayer for relief requests general and compensatory damages; punitive damages; damages for violations of the Commercial Code; and injunctive and declaratory relief. In sum, there is no evidence on this record that the matter will necessarily result in a verdict below $25,000 (or $35,000 if the January 1, 2024, amendment to CCP 85 applies).

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.
